name: Italian Game
description: One of the oldest openings in chess. White develops the bishop to c4, targeting the vulnerable f7 square and fighting for center control.

moves.commentary: King's pawn opening — claims the center and opens diagonals for the bishop and queen.
Black mirrors the center occupation, leading to an open game.
Develops the knight to its best square, attacking the e5 pawn.
Defends e5 with the knight while developing toward the center.
The Italian Bishop — targets f7, the weakest square in Black's position.
Black mirrors, placing the bishop on an active diagonal aiming at f2.
Prepares d4 to build a strong pawn center. The quiet approach — Giuoco Piano means 'quiet game'.
Develops the knight and counterattacks the e4 pawn.
Strikes the center as planned. White aims for a classical pawn duo on d4+e4.
Black captures to challenge White's center and open lines.
Recaptures with the pawn, establishing the ideal e4+d4 center.
Black checks and forces White to deal with the pin before developing further.
Blocks the check while developing. After the exchange, White will have a strong center.
Black exchanges to simplify and reduce White's attacking potential.
Recaptures with the knight, keeping pieces active and maintaining the center.
King's pawn opening — claims the center and opens diagonals.
Black occupies the center symmetrically.
Develops and attacks e5.
Defends e5 with the knight.
The Italian Bishop targets f7.
Black develops actively on the same diagonal.
The Evans Gambit! Sacrifices a pawn to deflect the bishop and gain time for a powerful center.
Black accepts the gambit. Declining with Bb6 is also playable but less common.
Attacks the bishop and prepares d4. White aims for rapid central expansion.
Retreats to a5 keeping pressure on the c3-pawn and eyeing the kingside.
White builds the ideal pawn center. The gambit pawn is well invested.
Black captures, opening the center.
White castles before recapturing. Development and king safety take priority over material.
Black returns the pawn to block White's development and slow the attack.
Puts pressure on f7 and d3. White has excellent compensation with active pieces and open lines.
Claims the center with the king's pawn.
Black mirrors the central occupation.
Develops and attacks e5.
Defends e5 naturally.
Italian Bishop — aims at f7.
The Two Knights Defense! Instead of Bc5, Black counterattacks e4 immediately. More aggressive than the Giuoco Piano.
The Fried Liver Attack direction — White doubles up on f7 with the bishop and knight.
The only good defense! Black counterattacks in the center rather than passively defending f7.
Captures the pawn, opening the position.
The main line — Black chases the bishop while ignoring the d5 pawn. Counterattack over defense.
Checks the king to gain a tempo. White wants to hold the extra d5 pawn.
Blocks the check and attacks the bishop, gaining time to recapture on d5.
White opens lines, counting on the lead in development.
Recaptures, opening the b-file for the rook. Black has compensation with active piece play.
White retreats the bishop to safety. A sharp, double-edged middlegame lies ahead.
Opens with the king's pawn.
Black responds symmetrically.
Develops the knight toward the center.
Defends e5.
Italian Bishop setup.
The Hungarian Defense — a solid but passive choice. Black avoids tactical complications of Bc5 or Nf6.
White takes advantage of Black's passive setup to seize space immediately.
Supports e5 and prepares to develop the light-squared bishop.
Opens the position while Black's pieces are still undeveloped.
Black recaptures, but the position is now more open than Black wanted.
Exchanging queens early. In this structure, White's development advantage matters more than the queen.
Black recaptures. The bishop is awkwardly placed on d8.
White develops with a clear advantage in piece activity. The plan is Bf4 or Be3 with easy development.
